questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between a CSV file and a database?
options: CSV files are slower because they use text encoding
A database provides ACID guarantees, a query language, concurrency control, and crash recovery
Databases are always faster than file-based storage
CSV files cannot store more than 1 GB of data
questions.explanation: A flat file like CSV offers none of the four ACID properties, has no query language beyond grep, no concurrency control, and relies on the application for crash recovery. A database engine provides all of these as built-in services.

questions.question: In a database engine's layered architecture, which component sits directly above the disk manager and caches recently used pages in memory?
options: The executor
The access methods
The buffer pool
The transaction manager
questions.explanation: The buffer pool sits right above the disk manager and caches disk pages (typically 8 KB or 16 KB each) in memory to avoid expensive disk reads. It uses a page replacement policy (LRU, clock, ARC) to decide which pages to evict.

questions.question: According to the RUM conjecture, which statement is true?
options: A storage engine can optimize all three of read speed, update speed, and memory overhead simultaneously
A storage engine can optimize for at most two of read speed, update speed, and memory overhead
A storage engine must always optimize for read speed first
The RUM conjecture only applies to relational databases
questions.explanation: The RUM conjecture states you can optimize at most two of Read, Update, and Memory. A B-tree optimizes for reads and memory (compact pages), an LSM-tree for updates and memory, and a hash index for reads and updates — but none can achieve all three.

questions.question: In the Bitcask storage model, how is the in-memory hash index rebuilt after a crash?
options: The index is written to a separate file on every PUT and read back on startup
The index is reconstructed by replaying the append-only log from beginning to end
The index is rebuilt from a network backup
The index is not recoverable after a crash
questions.explanation: On startup, Bitcask scans the append-only log sequentially from the first byte to the last, recording the latest offset for each key. This rebuilds the entire hash index without any additional metadata — the log is the source of truth.

questions.question: What is the Write-Ahead Log (WAL) primarily used for?
options: Improving SELECT query performance through caching
Ensuring durability: before a page is modified, a record of the change is written to a sequential log that can be replayed after a crash
Storing the entire database in a log format instead of pages
Logging SQL queries for audit purposes
questions.explanation: The WAL ensures durability (the D in ACID). Before any database page modification hits the data file, a WAL record describing the change is written and fsynced. On crash recovery, the WAL is replayed to restore the database to its last committed state.

questions.question: A database's query optimizer chooses a plan by:
options: Picking the plan that matches a fixed template
Using a random search through possible plans
Estimating the cost of alternative plan trees using table statistics and picking the cheapest
Always using a sequential scan for simplicity
questions.explanation: The optimizer enumerates alternative execution strategies (different join orders, index choices, access methods), estimates the cost of each using table statistics (row count, selectivity, index size), and selects the plan with the lowest estimated total cost.
